Assignment 4 (Data Visualization)

Instructions

  1. You may talk to a friend, discuss the questions and potential directions for solving them. However, you need to write your own solutions and code separately, and not as a group activity.

  2. Do not write your name on the assignment.

  3. Write your code in the Code cells and your answer in the Markdown cells of the Jupyter notebook. Ensure that the solution is written neatly enough to understand and grade.

  4. Use Quarto to print the .ipynb file as HTML. You will need to open the command prompt, navigate to the directory containing the file, and use the command: quarto render filename.ipynb --to html. Submit the HTML file.

  5. The assignment is worth 100 points, and is due on 4th November 2022 at 11:59 pm.

  6. Some questions in this assignment do not have a single correct answer. As data visualization is subject to interpretation, any logically sound answer / explanation is acceptable.

  7. There is a bonus question worth 30 points. However, there is no partial credit for the bonus question. You will get 30 or 0. If everything is correct, you can score 130 out of 100 in the assignment.

Exploring factors associated with profitability of a movie

In this assignment we’ll attempt to find the factors (or variables) that make a movie profitable.

Read the movies data from here, in a Pandas DataFrame named as movies_data. The profit of movie is defined as:

\[profit = Worldwide \ Gross - Production \ Budget\]

Code 
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
movies_data = pd.read_json('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vega/vega-datasets/master/data/movies.json')
Code 
movies_data.head()
Title US Gross Worldwide Gross US DVD Sales Production Budget Release Date MPAA Rating Running Time min Distributor Source Major Genre Creative Type Director Rotten Tomatoes Rating IMDB Rating IMDB Votes

3 Let's Talk About Sex 373615.0 373615.0 NaN 300000.0 Sep 11 1998 None NaN Fine Line None Comedy None None 13.0 NaN NaN
4 Slam 1009819.0 1087521.0 NaN 1000000.0 Oct 09 1998 R NaN Trimark Original Screenplay Drama Contemporary Fiction None 62.0 3.4 165.0
Code 
# Adding profit column to the dataframe
movies_data['Profit'] = movies_data['Worldwide Gross'] - movies_data['Production Budget']
movies_data.head()
Title US Gross Worldwide Gross US DVD Sales Production Budget Release Date MPAA Rating Running Time min Distributor Source Major Genre Creative Type Director Rotten Tomatoes Rating IMDB Rating IMDB Votes Profit

Time trend

Let us analyze if the profitability of a movie is associated with the time of its release.

Month of release

Make an appropriate plot to visualize the mean profit of movies released each month.

Hint:

  1. Use the Pandas function to_datetime() to convert Release Date to a datetime datatype.

  2. Use the library datetime to extract the month from Release Date.

(6 points)

Code 
import datetime as dt
Code 
movies_data['release_month'] = pd.DatetimeIndex(movies_data['Release Date']).month
Code 
import seaborn as sns
ax = sns.barplot(data=movies_data, x='release_month', y ='Profit')
ax.set(xlabel = 'Release Month', ylabel = 'Mean Profit')
ax.figure.set_figwidth(15)

Based on the plot, which seasons have been the most and least profitable (on an average) for a movie release. Don’t worry about the exact start and end date of seasons. Don’t perform any computations. Just make comments based on the plot. You can use seasons such as early summer, late spring etc.

(2 points)

Based on the plot, late spring/early summer is the most profitable season for a movie release. Early/mid fall seems to be the most unprofitable. On its own, January seems to have the lowest profitiablitiy, but as an entire season, I’d argue early/mid fall is less profitable than mid-winter.

Month of release with number of movies in each genre

Now that we know the most profitable season for releasing movies, let us visualize if some genres are more popular during certain seasons.

Use the code below to create a new column called genre.

Code 
#Combining Major Genre
movies_data['genre'] = movies_data['Major Genre'].apply(lambda x:'Comedy' if x!=None and 'Comedy' in x else 'Horror' if x!=None and 'Thriller' in x else 'Action/Adventure' if x!=None and ('Action' in x or 'Adventure' in x) else 'Musical/Western' if x!=None and ('Musical' in x or 'Western' in x or 'Concert' in x) else x)

Make an appropriate plot to visualize the number of movies released for each genre in each calendar month.

(8 points)

Hint:

  1. Use barplot() with estimator as len

  2. Use the hue argument

Code 
ax = sns.barplot(data=movies_data, x='release_month', y = 'Profit', hue = 'genre', estimator = len)
ax.set(xlabel = 'Release Month', ylabel='Number of movies released')
ax.figure.set_figwidth(15)

Based on the above plot, which genre is the most popular during the most profitable season of release? And which genre is the most popular during the least profitable season of release?

(2 points)

During the most profitable season of release (late spring/early summer), Action/Adventure movies see the highest number of releases. During the least profitable season of release (early/mid fall), dramas see the highest number of releases.

Month of release with proportion of movies in each genre

Visualize the proportion of movies in each genre for each month of release.

Use the code below to re-arrange your data that will help with creating the visualization

Code 
genre_proportion_release_month = pd.crosstab(index=movies_data['release_month'],
                             columns=movies_data['genre'],
                             normalize="index")
genre_proportion_release_month.head()
genre Action/Adventure Comedy Documentary Drama Horror Musical/Western
release_month
1 0.179310 0.310345 0.006897 0.262069 0.200000 0.041379
2 0.173267 0.311881 0.019802 0.277228 0.198020 0.019802
3 0.213043 0.369565 0.004348 0.217391 0.178261 0.017391
4 0.166667 0.390351 0.017544 0.184211 0.223684 0.017544
5 0.385714 0.257143 0.023810 0.176190 0.123810 0.033333

Hint:

  1. Make a 100% stacked barplot with the Pandas plot() function

  2. Use the argument bbox_to_anchor with the Matplotlib function legend() to place the legend outside the plot area.

(8 points)

Code 
ax = genre_proportion_release_month.plot.bar(stacked=True,ylabel = 'Proportion of genres',figsize=(15, 10), xlabel = 'Month')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter('{x:,.0f}')
ax.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1,1))
<matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0xa101469400>

Which genre is the most popular during the month of May, and which one is the most popular during December?

(2 points)

Action/Adventure movies are most popular during May, and Dramas are most popular during December.

Year of release with genre

Make an appropriate figure to visualize the average profit of movies of each genre for each year. Consider only the movies released from 1991 to 2010. Also show the 95% confidence interval in the average profit.

Hint:

  1. Use the library datetime to extract year from Release Date.

  2. Use the Seaborn Facetgrid() object.

  3. A figure can have multiple subplots. Put the figure for each genre in a separate subplot.

(6 points)

Code 
movies_data_years_filtered = movies_data_years_filtered[movies_data_years_filtered['release_year'].between(1990, 2010)]
Code 
movies_data['release_year'] = pd.DatetimeIndex(movies_data['Release Date']).year
movies_data_years_filtered = movies_data[movies_data['release_year'].between(1990, 2010)]
Code 
a = sns.FacetGrid(movies_data_years_filtered, col = 'genre',col_wrap=3,height=3.5,aspect = 1)
a.map(sns.lineplot,'release_year','Profit')
a.set_axis_labels("Release Year", "Profit")
a.add_legend()
<seaborn.axisgrid.FacetGrid at 0xa10007d790>

Based on the figure above, which genre’s profitabiltiy seems to be increasing over the years, and which genre has the least uncertainty in profit for most of the years.

(2 points)

Action/Adventure movies’ profit seems to be increasing over the years, while Documentaries have had the least uncertainty in profit for most of the years.

Associations

Pairplot / heatmap

Make a pairplot and heatmap of all the continuous variables in the data.

(8 points)

Code 
# dropping release month and release year for the pairplot:
movies_no_release_month_or_year = movies_data.drop(['release_month', 'release_year'], axis=1)
Code 
sns.pairplot(movies_no_release_month_or_year)
<seaborn.axisgrid.PairGrid at 0xa113113640>

Code 
sns.set(rc={'figure.figsize':(12,10)})
sns.heatmap(movies_data.corr(), annot=True)
<AxesSubplot:>

Are there any trends that you can see in the pairplot, but not in the heatmap?

(2 points)

Yes. Overall, the pairplot can show deeper intricacies of trends, while heatmaps will give insight on the most general trends between two variables. The pairplot can also show non-linear correlations, while heatmaps will only refelect linear correlations. For example, IMDB votes and IMDB rating appear to be correlated exponentially based on the pairplot, while the heatmap only reflects the linear association between the two.

Based on the plots in 2(a)(i), which variables are associated with profit?

(2 points)

Based off the heatmap, US Gross and Worldwide gross are highly positively correlated with profit, while US DVD sales, IMDB votes, and production budget are slightly correlated with profit.

Among the variables listed in 2(a)(iii), select a subset of variables such that none of them are highly associated with each other. The rest of the variables identified in 2(a)(iii) are redundant with regard to association with profit.

(2 points)

US DVD sales, IMDB votes, and production budget are all associated, but not highly (all correlation coefficients are less than .7).

Nested associations

Use the code below to create some new columns.

Code 
movies_data['screenplay'] = movies_data.Source.apply(lambda x:'Non-original' if x!='Original Screenplay' else x)
movies_data['rating'] = movies_data['MPAA Rating'].apply(lambda x:'R rated' if x=='R' else 'Not R rated')
movies_data['fiction'] = movies_data['Creative Type'].apply(lambda x:'Contemporary' if x=='Contemporary Fiction' else 'other')

Make an appropriate figure to visualize the association of the number of IMDB votes with profit for each genre (use the variable genre). Which genre has the highest association between profit and IMDB votes?

(8 points)

Code 
sns.lmplot(
    data=movies_data, x="IMDB Votes", y="Profit",
    col="genre")
<seaborn.axisgrid.FacetGrid at 0xa108f04a90>

Action/adventure has the highest association between profit and IMDB votes, with the horror genre as a close second, as shown by the trendlines.

Make an appropriate figure to visualize the association between the number of IMDB votes and profit, for each combination of the fiction type (use the variable fiction) and the movie rating (use the variable rating).

For which combination of fiction and rating categories do you observe the highest association between IMDB votes and profit?

(8 points)

Hint: Use row and col attributes of the Seaborn Facetgrid() object.

Code 
sns.lmplot(
    data=movies_data, x="IMDB Votes", y="Profit",
    col="fiction", row="rating"
)
<seaborn.axisgrid.FacetGrid at 0xa101528310>

‘Non R rated’ movies in the ‘other’ category for fiction have the highest correlation between IMDB votes and profit, based on the trendline.

Profit based on movie director

Consider the directors who have directed more than 10 movies (based on the dataset). Make a horizontal barplot that shows the mean profit of the movies of these directors along with the 95% confidence interval. Sort the bars of the barplot such that the director with the highest mean profit is at the top.

If the dataset director_with_more_than_10_movies has only those movies that correspond to directors with more than 10 movies, then the following code will give you the order in which the names of the directors must appear in the barplot:

(8 points)

Code 
director_count = movies_data['Director'].value_counts()
Code 
director_count_tenplus = director_count[director_count>10]
names = director_count_tenplus.index.tolist()
Code 
director_with_more_than_10_movies = movies_data[movies_data['Director'].isin(names)]
director_with_more_than_10_movies[['Director','Profit']].groupby('Director').mean().sort_values(by = 'Profit',
                                            ascending= False).index.to_list()
['Steven Spielberg',
 'Robert Zemeckis',
 'Tim Burton',
 'Sam Raimi',
 'Ron Howard',
 'Steven Soderbergh',
 'Tony Scott',
 'Ridley Scott',
 'Richard Donner',
 'Joel Schumacher',
 'Martin Scorsese',
 'Barry Levinson',
 'Robert Rodriguez',
 'Clint Eastwood',
 'Oliver Stone',
 'Francis Ford Coppola',
 'Brian De Palma',
 'Renny Harlin',
 'Woody Allen',
 'Spike Lee']
Code 
ax = sns.barplot(data=director_with_more_than_10_movies, x='Profit', y ='Director', order =
                director_with_more_than_10_movies[['Director','Profit']].groupby('Director').mean().sort_values(by = 'Profit',
                                            ascending= False).index.to_list())
ax.set(ylabel = 'Mean Profit')
ax.figure.set_figwidth(15)

Based on the above plot, which director has the highest mean profitability, and which one has the highest variation in profitability?

(2 points)

Steven Spielberg has the highest mean profitability, while Sam Raimi has the highest variation in profitability.

Distributions

Distribution of profit based on genre (boxplots)

Make boxplots to visualize the distribution of profit based on genre. Based on the plot, which genre has the most profitable movies?

(6 points)

Code 
#the outliers are quite extreme for some of the genres, so I'm going to leave them out
sns.boxplot(data = movies_data,x = 'genre', y = 'Profit', showfliers = False)
<AxesSubplot:xlabel='genre', ylabel='Profit'>

Based on the boxplot, action/adventure is the most profitable genre, as its meadian profit is higher than the other genres, as well as the upper range of its interquartile range.

Which genre has the most variation in profit, and which one has the least?

(2 points)

Action/adventure movies have the most variation in profit, while documentaries have the least variation in profit.

Distribution of profit based on genre (density plots)

Make density plots of profit based on genre. Adjust the limit on the horizonal axis, so that the plots are clearly visible.

(6 points)

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
sns.set(font_scale = 1.4)
sns.displot(movies_data, x = 'Profit',kind = 'kde', col = 'genre')
plt.ylim(0, None)
plt.xlim(None, 1e9)
(-449053241.13259804, 1000000000.0)

What additional insight / trend can you seen in the above plot that you cannot see with the boxplots?

(2 points)

We can see that the density plots of all of the genres are unimodal and right skewed, revealing that the mean profit is higher than the median because of outlying values on the positive side of the distribution. The density plots also show the sample size that each genre has for its data, wheras the boxplots do not. For example, we can see that drama movies are made with the most frequency, while documentaries are made with the least frequency

Insights

From all the visualizations above, describe the insights you get about the factors associated with the profitability of a movie.

Also, elaborate on the extent to which these trends can be generalized. For example, comment on whether these trends be generalized to the current time and all the Hollywood movies? If not, is there any time period or type of movie to which these trends can be applicable?

(4+ 4 points)

Firstly, there are some variables that seem to be positively correlated with profit: US Gross, Worldwide gross, US DVD sales, IMDB votes, and production budget. US Gross and Worldwide gross can be ignored, as it is obvious that increases in profit would also result in increases in the US and Worldwide Gross variables. The three other variables (US DVD sales, IMDB votes, and production budget) being correlated makes sense. High production budget would most likely result in higher levels of advertisement and an increased amount of high value actors (and other important set members), both of which would increase public engagement with the film. One would also expect that a high profit would also correlate to higher DVD sales. An interesting note: while IMDB votes are positively correlated with profit (high levels engagement with a movie on IMDB probably means that a high number of people watched the movie in the first place), higher IMDB ratings and Rotten Tomato scores are much less positively correlated with profit. In the movie business, it seems that the quanity of interactions is to be desired over the quality of interactions.

If one were to make a movie with the hopes of maximizing profit, I would give them a few pieces of advice: first, if profit is the only goal, then make an action/adventure movie. Based on the boxplots, while action/adventure movies might arguably have the highest risk in that they have the highest potential to lose money (based on the 25th percentile), they have the highest upside in profit, by far. Furthermore, their median profit and interquartile range is significantly above all of the other movies, meaning that one would be more likely to make a high profit through the release of an action adventure movie than any other genre. To lose money in the production of an action/adventure movie would be unlucky, based on this data.

To further maximize profit, a wise decision, based off of the barplots of the directors, would be to hire a well-seasoned director. Directors who have more than 10 movies under their belt seem to be quite likely to make a profit, as only two of those directors has a variance that stretches into the negative profit zone. Finally, one should look to release their movie in the late spring/early summer, as that period of time has been found to have the highest average profit.

These insights, especially the ones on action/adventure genres being profitable, seem to be applicable to the current day. While all the other genres have either been fairly stable or have even decreased in profitability since the 1990s, action/adventure movies have steadily increased in popularity, and that trend doesn’t seem to be subsiding. When one considers some of the blockbuster movie production companies, such as Marvel Studios, or Star Wars, it is clear that action/adventure movies continue to have a resounding grasp on the public’s intrigue. A similar argument could be made against producing a musical/western, as its profitability dropped significantly in the 1990s, and has been slightly erratic since.

The data visualizations that suggests seasoned directors are profitable makes sense, and would still be applied to Hollywood today. Experience always holds value, and that’s no different in the landscape of moviemaking.

Lastly, while these trends and insights could, and arguably should, influence one’s approach to producing a movie, it’s important to note that correlation does not imply causation. For example, releasing an action/adventure movie in May will not ensure that the movie will be profitable. This is just a trend, and there may be other counfounding variables that go into the success of a movie. So, while these trends can be generalized to the current time, it shouldn’t be done with complete certainty that following these trends will cause the desired outcome. We also don’t know the specifics of how the data was gathered. This dataset seems to be extensive, yet we cannot be completely sure about whether it is an accurate representation of all Hollywood movies, or movies in general.
